national_expectations.summary: The Germany shootout win reset expectations overnight: reaching the knockout round was already framed as historic, but eliminating a four-time champion is widely described as having already exceeded anything realistically hoped for. Coverage now treats the Round of 16 tie with France as pure bonus territory for Paraguay, with the pressure seen as squarely on the heavy favorites; coach Gustavo Alfaro has publicly cautioned that the squad must not overvalue the Germany result heading into a France side several levels above them on paper.

national_mood.summary: The mood is euphoric and described by multiple outlets as one of the country's biggest sporting nights in memory: after the shootout victory, crowds filled central Asuncion (the Palma street area became the focal point), with celebrations reported in streets, plazas and homes across the country and even among Paraguayans abroad. President Santiago Pena declared a national holiday for the following day, amplifying the result into a countrywide event rather than a purely sporting one. The bitter, divided tone that followed the group stage has been replaced by broad pride, though some coverage frames the celebration as tempered by an awareness that Paraguay remain heavy underdogs against France.

manager_situation.summary: Argentine head coach Gustavo Alfaro called the Germany win the biggest of his coaching career and framed it as vindication for a squad he says played with 'dignity' against a world power, dedicating it explicitly to earlier critics. In the same breath he has warned against reading too much into the result before France, telling reporters the team must not get carried away and stressing Paraguay still lack the experience ('bagaje') of a side used to going deep in tournaments. He remains the central figure of the team's story, now cast as the coach who orchestrated a genuine World Cup shock rather than merely as the man who got Paraguay back to the tournament.

historical_context.summary: Paraguay's 2010 run to the quarterfinals (a 1-0 loss to eventual champions Spain) remained their best-ever World Cup result and their only prior knockout-stage win came in that same edition. The 2026 shootout win over Germany is now reported as a new landmark in its own right: press coverage describes it as Germany's first-ever penalty shootout loss at a World Cup, ending a run in which the four-time champions had never previously lost a World Cup shootout. Against France specifically, Paraguay have never won in two prior World Cup meetings: a 7-3 defeat in the 1958 group stage, and a 1-0 loss to a 114th-minute Laurent Blanc golden goal in the 1998 Round of 16 (played in Lens), a match in which Jose Luis Chilavert -- since a public critic of Alfaro -- was Paraguay's goalkeeper.

dominant_storylines.storyline: A genuine World Cup shock: Paraguay's penalty-shootout win ended Germany's run of never having lost a World Cup shootout and is widely described as the tournament's first big knockout-round surprise, with even German outlets (per ABC Color's roundup of international press) calling it a national embarrassment.

Orlando Gill, the keeper who saved a nation's night: goalkeeper Orlando Gill, already named the tournament's best goalkeeper of the group stage, made decisive saves in the shootout and has become the individual face of the win, with reporting also highlighting a difficult personal backstory that has amplified his standing as a folk hero at home.

A national holiday and a country in the streets: President Santiago Pena declared the following Tuesday a national holiday, and reporting describes Asuncion's downtown and other cities erupting in impromptu, hours-long celebrations, turning a football result into a shared national event well beyond the sport.

Alfaro dedicates the win to the doubters, then pumps the brakes: the coach explicitly framed the result as an answer to critics from the group-stage debate (echoing the earlier Chilavert row), while in the same set of comments warning his squad against overvaluing a single result before a far tougher French test.

Cast as extreme underdogs against France: previews describe this as one of the round's biggest mismatches on paper -- France pre-tournament top-3 in the world versus Paraguay 41st -- with France having scored three or more goals in five straight World Cup matches and Kylian Mbappe tied for the tournament's top scorer.

A rematch Paraguay have never won: local and regional press are framing the France tie against the backdrop that Paraguay have lost both of their previous World Cup meetings with France, including the 1998 Round of 16 defeat to Laurent Blanc's golden goal, giving the match a 'revancha' (revenge) angle layered on top of the pure underdog story.

France publicly refuse to look past Paraguay: after the Germany result, Mbappe and French coverage have gone out of their way to praise Paraguay's resistance and warn against complacency, a notable shift in tone from the pure-mismatch framing of the pre-Germany previews.

attributed_quotes.quote: We didn't play well, and there are defeats that hurt and defeats that teach.
attributed_quotes.quote_original: No jugamos bien y hay derrotas que duelen y hay derrotas que enseñan.
attributed_quotes.context: Alfaro reflecting on the campaign after eliminating Germany, weighing the earlier group-stage loss to the US against the shootout win.

attributed_quotes.quote: We had very big plans for this World Cup. It's very difficult to disappoint again.
attributed_quotes.context: Havertz reacting after missing a penalty in the shootout that eliminated Germany.

attributed_quotes.quote: What I want to highlight from our team is how united we are.
attributed_quotes.context: Canale speaking after scoring Paraguay's winning penalty in the shootout against Germany.

attributed_quotes.quote: It's a team that must be taken seriously.
attributed_quotes.quote_original: Es un equipo que hay que tomar en serio.
attributed_quotes.context: Mbappe previewing the Round of 16 tie against Paraguay after their win over Germany.

attributed_quotes.quote: Paraguay never gives up! Holiday, damn it!
attributed_quotes.context: President Pena announcing a national holiday on social media immediately after Paraguay's shootout win over Germany.

local_availability_reporting.status_reported: Reported to still be in rehabilitation for a knee injury sustained in the closing stages of the Australia group match; as of July 1 he was doing light jogging to test his response, with fitness for the France match not confirmed.

gaps: Our DB ground facts record the Germany shootout as Paraguay winning 4-3; one AI-summarized fetch of an ESPN page rendered it as '5-4' and press accounts vary on exactly which Germany takers missed (some name Havertz, Woltemade and Tah; others only Havertz and Tah) and whether a Paraguay taker also missed before the winning kick. The 4-3 scoreline from our DB is treated as authoritative; the exact shootout kick-by-kick sequence is not stated as fact in this file and is deliberately kept general (multiple Germany misses, including Havertz) rather than asserting a specific order.

Minor spelling variance across outlets on the winning penalty-taker's name (Canale vs. a one-off 'Canali'); 'Jose Canale' is used here as the consistent spelling across Infobae, Telemundo and Mediotiempo.

No verbatim Alfaro quote specifically previewing the France match was located as of the 2026-07-01 cutoff; his available post-match comments concern the Germany result and general caution, not France-specific tactics or team news, so no France-specific manager quote is included.

Reported availability for Ramon Sosa (ankle) and the suspension status of Diego Gomez, both flagged ahead of the Germany match, were not re-confirmed by any source found for the France match; treated as unknown rather than assumed resolved.

No official FIFA/CONMEBOL/Paraguayan Football Association (APF) statement was located on mood, expectations, or squad availability; all national-mood and expectations material here is press-attributed (discovery tier), not fact-grade.

ABC Color and D10/Ultima Hora (Paraguay's leading national newspapers, each with dedicated sports desks) are not on the shared outlet allowlist; used here as 'other' per the allowlist's own guidance for under-covered federations -- both are mainstream, long-established national outlets, not fringe sources. Infobae, La Nacion (Argentina edition, distinct from any Paraguayan outlet of the same name), Mi Telefe/Noticias Argentinas, El Universo, El Colombiano, Statsniper and FIFA.com's editorial 'historial' article are similarly used as 'other' -- established regional/international outlets or FIFA's own editorial content, chosen given the limited Paraguay-specific coverage on the shared list.
